design_philosophy.core_loop: Progress through linear levels defeating enemy waves and bosses using acquired ninjutsu abilities (Ninja Arts), collecting hidden children as optional objectives
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Resource management of limited Ninja Arts uses against boss patterns that require specific ability sequencing; navigating crowded enemy layouts with limited mobility options
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Ninja vigilante rescuing kidnapped children from organized crime; progression as mastery of martial techniques
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Boss design philosophy mandates specific ability sequences rather than flexible approaches—final boss El Toro requires Fire Bomb → Fire Ring → Spider → Missiles in exact order, creating puzzle-like combat encounters. Optional child rescues carry no mechanical reward, emphasizing narrative motivation over gameplay incentive. Localization shifted from espionage narrative (Japanese) to anti-drug PSA (Western), creating two distinct design intentions.

difficulty.lives_system: Multiple lives per stage with checkpoint progression
difficulty.continues_system: Limited continues in international release (more generous in Japanese version)
difficulty.checkpointing: Per-stage checkpoints; final level truncated from 6 to 4 floors in international release, reducing overall difficulty
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Player selects which Ninja Art to use via Start menu during normal combat, but final boss removes this choice—requires predetermined ability sequence. Difficulty modulation through stage design changes between versions (removed cheap enemy placement, reduced final stage length in Western release)

progression.upgrade_system: Ninjutsu abilities (Ninja Arts) acquired after defeating each stage boss—Fire Bomb, Fire Ring, Spider, Missiles, plus others. Abilities unlocked sequentially, not selectable until earned
progression.unlock_mechanism: Stage-gated: new Ninja Art learned after boss defeat via cutscene showing master's training. Abilities assigned to different directional inputs (stand still vs. move left/right varies between versions)
replayability_hooks: Hidden children scattered throughout levels provide optional rescue objectives with no mechanical benefit
Stage select cheat codes in Japanese version enable non-linear access
Invincibility cheat code present in Japanese version
Regional differences create substantially different experiences (5 stages in Black Manta vs. 6 in Saizou; different bosses; truncated final level)
Boss art completely redrawn between versions suggesting different encounter designs

world_building_techniques: Cutscenes with character art establish antagonist El Toro and his organization
Inter-stage dialogue sequences between villain and henchmen
Level introductions with narrative hooks (protagonist receives notes from kidnapped child Taro)
NPC interrogation sequences showing captured mobsters
Completely redrawn art style for Western localization (realistic comic-book style vs. anime-esque Japanese version)
Regional narrative divergence: DRAT organization (drug runners/terrorists kidnapping children as dealers) in West vs. warmongers recruiting child soldiers in Japan

boss_design.philosophy: Pattern-locked encounters with fixed attack sequences and predetermined defeat methods. Boss encounters test ability sequencing knowledge rather than reactive skill. Final boss conceptually unique: holds hostage, requires ability-sequence puzzle solution, has minimal health (4 points) but multiple mechanical restrictions
notable_bosses: Level 1 Boss: Larger realistic sprite, stomping attacks, vulnerable only to high-jump shots (wave punch attack removed in international version)
Level 4 Boss (Level 3 in Black Manta): Electric Ghost in Japanese version vs. 'Voodoo Warrior' magician with skull staff in Western version—completely reimagined encounter
Round 3 Exclusive Boss (Japanese only): Bioweapon—giant floating eyeball-headed snake creature

Final Boss El Toro: Hostage-holding gunman requiring four-part Ninja Art sequence (Fire Bomb → Fire Ring → Spider → Missiles) in strict order. Loses hair after two hits. Takes damage from Shurikens only but if used, damages player or fully heals El Toro if already damaged. Invulnerable to mid-air shots unless Shurikens used. Collision = instant death. Player can select abilities during fight unlike other bosses
Penultimate encounter (Japanese version only): Players face all five previous bosses before final boss; international version reduces to one previous boss

faction_indicators: DRAT organization (Western localization): Drug Runners And Terrorists—anti-establishment criminal syndicate
Japanese unnamed organization: Militaristic arms dealers operating internationally, recruiting child soldiers
El Toro: Crime boss / warlord leader figure in both versions
Protagonist's dojo: Master-student hierarchy, traditional ninja discipline structure
danger_signals: Child kidnapping campaigns indicating organized crime reach
Weapons escalation (throwing stars → Ninja Arts → missiles)
Interrogation sequences showing captured enemies and extracted intelligence
First-person perspective segment in final stage suggesting disorientation/danger intensification
environmental_storytelling: Stage-based location progression through criminal underworld territories
Enemy variety reflecting organizational hierarchy (common mobsters vs. specialized bosses)
Rooftop sequences (Japanese version Stage 2) suggesting urban criminal infrastructure
Jukebox detail in final boss arena (Manta must shoot jukebox to trigger El Toro appearance—mundane object transformed into narrative trigger)
Regional art style choices reinforce narrative tone: comic-book realism vs. anime stylization creates different threat perception
